Scott Northwolf.author: Scott Northwolf
Scott Northwolf.text:
Recently I was on a coaching call with one of my clients and at a certain point I was going rounds and rounds trying to break one of his limiting beliefs and he just said to me "Brother, be simple. Get to the point."
I could tell how tired he was of me just repeating myself every time we'd disagree and I realized then...
"Wait a minute! Why is this not working? Why is this taking so long? Why do I feel like I'm speaking for ages and getting nowhere?"
Then it struck me even harder when I was on two separate calls with other coaches while we were talking about business.
They'd digress like crazy and it'd feel like pure ADHD every step of the conversation. Then I realized they were a mirror to my own behaviour and I could tell how tiring it was the whole deal.
Since then I have decided to try to keep it simple.
Condensed the biggest amount of high leverage knowledge in the shortest sentences possible and focus on actionable steps way more than on overexplaining super complex concepts just to satisfy my own ego and show just how smart I am (I am, and also the most humble man in the universe in case you haven't been able to tell by now).
Being comfortable speaking less and even remaining in silence it's an art and it brings you more power, not less.
Striking the balance is essential.
Speak too much and you'll come across weak and as if you have something to proof.
We all have something to proof but we must start by proving that to ourselves so it comes off naturally and it's projected from a place of abundance, groundness and relaxation instead of from a place of lack and fear.
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remeber: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

Scott Northwolf.author: Lea Newkirk
Scott Northwolf.text: As a yapper myself, I take my hat off to you for being able to come to this conclusion and to try to improve as well! This helped me learn so much, thank you!

Lea Newkirk.author: Lea Newkirk
Lea Newkirk.text:
A while ago, I learnt the concept of confirmation bias. Basically, the human tendency to only pay attention to information that proves what we already believe, while ignoring anything that doesn't. The way I act on this is, waking up, deciding it is already the best day ever and my job is to hunt for all the reasons why (at least 3), within minutes I already have so many reasons and I have not had a bad day since 😂 not because bad things don't happen (that's unrealistic) but because regardless, whatever I focus on will confirm what I already believe.
I am very curious, if anyone else does something similar, please share! I would love to hear about your experience and what usually confirms that the day will be an amazing one?

Lea Newkirk.author: Scott Northwolf
Lea Newkirk.text: Gratitude is the fastlane to abundance... abundance of life, of joy and of everything you can desire from a place of fullness intead of from a place of lack.

Tom Stoll.author: Scott Northwolf
Tom Stoll.text:
I agree completely.
First thing to understand is where people are amongst the 3 awareness levels.
Are they?
1-No awareness (they are in pain but don't know what's their problem).
2-Problem aware (they know they are in pain and know at least on a high level view what's their problem and starting to activeley look for solutions).
3-Solution awareness (know they have a problem and know what's the solution, now they are ready to take action on it).
Level 2 and 3 are the best parts.
That's the warmest leads you'll get.
Funnel structure:
1-At level one you call out their pain and attract them to your community (or YT channel or whatever).
2-At level 2 you get them inside your community and give them declarative knowledge so they start seeing what's their "enemy", their problem.
3-At level 3 you present them with the solution and they are ready to start taking actionable steps in order to solve it, usually this is best done on a sales' call already.
Delusion backed up just by words it's just delusion but delusion backed up by relentless action... that's called FAITH, brother! Remember: Duty, Honor and Pride!!!

Mohammed Attar.author: Mohammed Attar
Mohammed Attar.text:
I see people celebrate wins with screenshots all the time. But screenshots get buried. They disappear in the feed.
I decided to make my wins permanent. These are three 5-star reviews from my best clients. I framed them and hung them on my wall.
Why? Because I need to remind myself of the standard of work I provide. Every day.
And when I’m on a call, or making a post like this, it’s not just words. It’s physical proof of authority.
The Strategy:
Identify your top 3 wins.
Turn them into tangible, physical objects.
Use them to dominate your environment and build authority.
Your wins deserve to be permanent.
Call to Action: What is one win you’ve had this month that is worth of a frame? Post it below! 👇

Brandon Maloney.author: Brandon Maloney
Brandon Maloney.text: I've realized recently I'm not obsessed like I used to be with things. I would say I'm more productive, smarter, more skilled, but the absolute OBSESSION I had with sports, or training, or work, just simply isn't there to the degree it used to be at the moment.  My question to you is, how do you WANT more. How do you give yourself big goals that almost scare you, that make you crave working on them?  (Also funny picture of my when I was in middle school)

Brandon Maloney.author: Scott Northwolf
Brandon Maloney.text: 3 things:1-Goal setting, drive vacuums identification and reverse engineering of your goal into actionable steps.2-After identifying your goal get inspiration and information about it (e.g. if you want to become a millionaire read the biographies of Andrew Carnegie and Rockefeller, if you want to become a military genious read Alexander's Campagins by Arian and Napoleon: A life).3-Identify your current beliefs with regards to the components of reality that you are struggling with and flip those beliefs in their head, build then a Self-Transcendence Document or Alchemy of Self Workbook.If you want me to explain in more depth those things just shoot me a DM. I know the third one is the "weirdest" one but it requires a LOT of explanation so it makes sense...The logical chain is like this:Experiences form beliefs.Beliefs form your identity.Your identity informs your paradigm (the lenses through which you see the world and interpret it).Your paradigm will inform your percieved options.Your percieved options will inform your actions (you can only make a decision out of the percieved options you believe are available to you).Your actions will solidify (only temporarily) into your behaviour (habits, routines [intentional or unintentional]).Your behaviour will determine your results.

Brandon Maloney.author: Brandon Maloney

Michael Pluszek.author: Michael Pluszek
Michael Pluszek.text: I posted a question about identity earlier in Synthesizer community, and it sparked some great conversations and perspectives.  Now another question came up that I’m curious about.  What is identity… and what is soul?  What’s the difference?  And how does a person actually know what is what?  Would love to hear your reflections and perspectives.

Michael Pluszek.author: Scott Northwolf
Michael Pluszek.text: Alright! So let me break this down.For clarity and simplicities' sake I'll omit some details and break it down into a simplistic model developed by the ancient Hermetics.Reality is structured in different layers of organization.1-Source consciousness (the void, the one, what ancient Greeks in the Pythagorean tradition used to call the Monad).2-The Mental Plain (The Duat).3-The causal plain (where there's an observer, an object and a subject, you need three elements to have an experience, you need an object, a subject and an option, an experience itself).4-The Astral Plain (what's called the Quantum Field of Possibilities, where ideas, language, feelings and imagination exist as components of reality we can percieve).5-Physical reality (the manifestation and integration of all the above components).To understand identity and the soul we need to understand first this structure because it's foundational to the understanding that flows from this. If we don't take this as the basis the rest will not make sense and to explain why this is so it will take... well, a lot of time, so let's just suspend disbelief and accept this framework as a precodition for the following knowledge to unfold itself.If you want to get the full explanation just let me know and I'll write the second part of this...

Krista Melanson.author: Krista Melanson
Krista Melanson.text:
One thing I’ve really enjoyed about spending time in Synthesizer is the energy in the room.
There are a lot of builders here — people thinking deeply about offers, systems, momentum, and the long game of building something meaningful.
I’ve learned a lot just by listening to the conversations.
At the same time, I sometimes realize I’m observing the room from a slightly different vantage point.
Most of the people here are younger entrepreneurs building businesses.
Meanwhile, I’m a little further down the life arc, and my work focuses on helping people build strong, supportive relationships.
So I often find myself quietly watching the conversations about growth, focus, and grinding forward.
From where I sit, something eventually becomes very clear for most people:
At some point, the thing that either supports or destabilizes everything you’re building isn’t strategy.
It’s your relationships.
Timing plays a big role in when people realize that.
So I’m curious:
Have you ever noticed that the people who benefit most from what you do often haven’t reached the moment in their life where they can see it yet?

Krista Melanson.author: Scott Northwolf
Krista Melanson.text: I love this reflection... precisely today I was thinking about the relationships I have built with my clients over the last couple of years and how crucial those relationships have been for all the incredible things we have built together and we are still currently building and I realize that just like on an adventure book or a story... these are my allies on this epic journey and we are facing the unknown together and shooting for massive goals, staying loyal to each other and trusting this vision bigger and higher than any of us individually but that brings profound meaning and direction to our lives and individual stories...

Christy Sanderson.author: Christy Sanderson
Christy Sanderson.text:
There was a time when I could articulate powerful ideas.
Clarity. Discipline. Identity. Structure. Consistency.
I could write about them. Teach them. Quote them.
But there was a quiet gap between what I said and what I had actually tested under pressure.
I remember one particular week — overwhelmed, financially stretched, emotionally tired. I had been speaking about consistency and structure publicly… but privately, my own systems were fragile.
That’s when it hit me.
It’s easy to repost wisdom when life is calm.
It’s different to live it when life pushes back.
My pain point wasn’t hypocrisy.
It was realization.
I had absorbed powerful ideas from books, podcasts, communities — and I believed them. But belief is not embodiment.
Pressure reveals what you’ve actually integrated.
So I made a decision.
I stopped sharing ideas I hadn’t personally run through fire.
I stopped echoing what sounded impressive.
I started asking:
Does this hold up when I’m tired?
Does this work when money is tight?
Does this help when doubt creeps in?
Does this produce results consistently?
And something changed.
My voice got quieter — but stronger.
My content got simpler — but sharper.
Because now, when I say something works, it’s not because it sounds right.
It’s because I’ve watched it survive stress.
There’s a difference between information and proof.
Reposting spreads ideas.
Pressure-testing builds authority.
And authority built through lived experience can’t be shaken by trends.
What idea have you personally pressure-tested that most people only repost?

Christy Sanderson.author: Scott Northwolf
Christy Sanderson.text:
Raw action solves it all.
Goal -> Daily actionable plan (reverse engineered) -> Outputs -> Feedback and reiteration (tracking)
Also understanding yourself and how your mind works makes a massive difference because of the following:
Experiences create beliefs, beliefs conform your identity (there are three kinds of beliefs but I'm not getting into that now, ask @Ethan Shahan lol 😂), your identity builds your paradigm, your paradigm (the lenses through which you actually observe components of reality [tangible and intangible]) will inform your percieved options and from your percieved options you will make a decision and that decision will turn into action, enough action will install on your unconscious mind a certain program as a behaviour solidifying it (not permanently, neuroplasticity is a thing), with enough awareness you can have a saying to make such behaviour (routine) intentional or unintentional.
Thank you for this post. I really appreciate it.
I wouldn't have believed all of this if I hadn't tested it myself and I am so fucking glad I have always been a mad scientist of sorts running experiements on myself and choosing to try things out to see if they actually work. To it I owe my success.

Christy Sanderson.author: Christy Sanderson
Christy Sanderson.text: @Christian Rogers Yes, this is so true. A lot of people can repeat advice or concepts, but everything changes when you actually apply it in real life. Action exposes what works, what doesn’t, and what needs adjustment. I appreciate your honesty about learning that lesson firsthand. That kind of self-awareness is valuable because it helps others realize that growth is a process. Thank you for sharing your experience and reinforcing the importance of execution.
